---
title: "Child care - Colorado Sun"
output: html_notebook
---


This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code. 

Try executing this chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Cmd+Shift+Enter*. 

```{r}
plot(cars)
```

Add a new chunk by clicking the *Insert Chunk* button on the toolbar or by pressing *Cmd+Option+I*.

When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output will be saved alongside it (click the *Preview* button or press *Cmd+Shift+K* to preview the HTML file). 

The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the editor. Consequently, unlike *Knit*, *Preview* does not run any R code chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the editor is displayed.

```{r} 

# setting up 

install.packages("janitor")
install.packages("tidyverse")
 install.packages("sqldf")
 install.packages("tidycensus")

library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(sqldf)
library(tidycensus)



```

```{r}

# To address any dplyr conflicts within tidyverse
  # https://conflicted.r-lib.org/ 

 install.packages("conflicted")
      library(conflicted)
      
      library(dplyr)
      conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter)

```


```{r}
# Note on using the tidycensus package
  # Background: https://carolinademography.cpc.unc.edu/2022/05/16/story-recipe-how-to-obtain-census-data-using-r-tidycensus/
  # My API key: ea07b3cb1702529c0736fed80af4412aaaf0bb8d

census_api_key("ea07b3cb1702529c0736fed80af4412aaaf0bb8d", install = TRUE)


```
```{r}

# Obtained data through Colorado Kids but can also be downloaded from the CO Dept of Early Childhood here: https://data.colorado.gov/Early-childhood/Colorado-Licensed-Child-Care-Facilities-Report/a9rr-k8mu/about_data

# Import data 

  # Old Dec. 2025 data:

    COLicensedChildCareReportForUpload_2025_12_in_ <- read_csv("COLicensedChildCareReportForUpload_2025-12(in).csv")
    View(COLicensedChildCareReportForUpload_2025_12_in_)

  # New Feb. 15, 2026 data directly from the state that I will be using to re-run the analysis to make sure the map is as current as possible
    
    library(readr)
    Colorado_Licensed_Child_Care_Facilities_Report_20260215 <- read_csv("Colorado_Licensed_Child_Care_Facilities_Report_20260215.csv")
    View(Colorado_Licensed_Child_Care_Facilities_Report_20260215)
        
# Clean it 

licensed_child_care <- Colorado_Licensed_Child_Care_Facilities_Report_20260215 %>% 
  clean_names()



```

```{r}

# Group by number of licensed spots by county and then export 

    # Least spots to most
      
       county_child_care_capacity <- licensed_child_care %>% 
        group_by(county) %>% 
        summarise(sum_child_care_spots = sum(total_licensed_capacity, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
        arrange(sum_child_care_spots)


    # Check it with SQL 

       sqldf("SELECT county, sum(total_licensed_capacity)
            FROM licensed_child_care
            GROUP BY county
            ORDER BY sum(total_licensed_capacity)")
       
       # Same answer

# Export
      
    county_child_care_capacity %>% write_csv("county_child_care_capacity.csv", na = "") 



```


```{r}

# Break out just the spots from counties with the four fewest spots - and county with only one facility

  # Jackson 

    jackson_child_care_spots <- licensed_child_care %>% 
            filter(county == "Jackson")

  # Dolores

     dolores_child_care_spots <- licensed_child_care %>% 
            filter(county == "Dolores")
     
  # Kiowa
     
      kiowa_child_care_spots <- licensed_child_care %>% 
            filter(county == "Kiowa")

  # San Juan 
      
      san_juan_child_care_spots <- licensed_child_care %>% 
            filter(county == "San Juan")
      
  # Crowley
      
      crowley_child_care_spots <- licensed_child_care %>% 
            filter(county == "Crowley")
      
# Export
      
      jackson_child_care_spots %>% write_csv("jackson_child_care_spots.csv", na = "") 
     
      dolores_child_care_spots %>% write_csv("dolores_child_care_spots.csv", na = "") 
 
      kiowa_child_care_spots %>% write_csv("kiowa_child_care_spots.csv", na = "") 

      san_juan_child_care_spots %>% write_csv("san_juan_child_care_spots.csv", na = "") 

```

```{r}


# Looks like there are only one or two child care centers for entire counties, especially in San Juan and Jackson counties. 
  # So, I wanted to count the number of unique licenses per county 

   facility_county_count <- licensed_child_care %>% 
      distinct(license_number, .keep_all = TRUE) %>% 
      group_by(county) %>%
      summarize(count = n()) %>% 
      arrange(count)  

  # Three counties only have one child care facility:
  
    # Crowley                     1
    # Jackson                     1
    # San Juan                     1
  
  # Verify in SQL:

      sqldf("SELECT county, count(license_number)
            FROM licensed_child_care
            GROUP BY county
            ORDER BY count(license_number)")
        
          # SQL verified - same thing found

# Export
      
    #  facility_county_count %>% write_csv("facility_county_count.csv", na = "") 
     

```

```{r}

# Spots by county for infant, toddler and preschool 

  pre_k_total <- licensed_child_care %>% 
        rowwise() %>% 
        mutate(pre_k_total = sum(c_across(licensed_center_infant_capacity:licensed_center_preschool_capacity), na.rm = TRUE))


pre_k_county_child_care_capacity <- pre_k_total %>% 
        group_by(county) %>% 
        summarise(sum_pre_k_total_spots = sum(pre_k_total, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
        arrange(sum_pre_k_total_spots)

# Export:

   pre_k_county_child_care_capacity %>% write_csv("pre_k_county_child_care_capacity.csv", na = "") 

  # Sedgwick is appearing as 0 spots here, but one facility lists 40 spots in the licensed_preschool_and_school_age_capacity column. 

    # This is a data reporting issue. State PIO Chynna Roberts said each center can report their licensed spots however they would like. 

  # "Child care centers may have their licensed capacity reported in a couple of ways. Some centers are licensed for a combined capacity of both preschool and school-age children, and this total is reported together. In contrast, programs licensed exclusively as preschool or school-age centers report their capacity separately, as they only serve children within those specific age groups."

```




```{r}

# Want to pull out the kids 7 and under in each county
  # Then subtract the total kids pop minus the number of licensed spots

# Census API

      decennial_2020_vars <- load_variables(
        year = 2020, 
        "pl", 
        cache = TRUE
      )
      
      # 2010 Decennial Census Variables
      decennial_2010_vars <- load_variables(
        year = 2010, 
        "pl", 
        cache = TRUE
      )
      
      # 2016 - 2020 5 Year American Community Survey (ACS) Variables
      
      acs_20_vars = load_variables(
        year = 2020, 
        "acs5",
        cache = TRUE
      )

    # Defaults to the most recent ACS and as the 5-year unless otherwise noted
        # 2024 ACS was recently added
      
      get_acs(
        geography = "county",
        table = "B09001",
        state = "CO",
        year = 2024
      )
      
        # That worked! This was the inspo and it matches which is great
          # https://censusreporter.org/data/table/?table=B09001&geo_ids=04000US08,050|04000US08&primary_geo_id=04000US08#valueType|estimate
      
        # Another helpful resource:
          # https://nicar.r-journalism.com/2024/

    # It is not perfect and astoundingly annoying but CDEC says someone in preschool could be 2.5 to 7 years old. So I need to include that age range 
      # From Chynna Roberts (chynna.roberts@state.co.us) 
    
         # Here’s a brief explanation of the columns you referenced:
    
            # Licensed Home Capacity: birth to 18 years
            # Licensed Center Infant Capacity: 6 weeks to 18 months
            # Licensed Center Toddler Capacity: 12 months - 36 months
            # Licensed Center Preschool Capacity: 2.5 years - 7 years
            # Licensed Preschool And School Age Capacity: 2 years and older
            # Licensed School Age Capacity: 5 years and older
            # Licensed Nyo Capacity: 5 years and older
            # Licensed Resident Camp Capacity: children who have completed kindergarten or are 6 years of age or older

      # Also can download the data and the definitions here:
        # https://data.colorado.gov/Early-childhood/Colorado-Licensed-Child-Care-Facilities-Report/a9rr-k8mu/about_data

# To see what variables we need
      
    vars <- load_variables(2024, "acs5")
      
    View(vars)
    
  # I searched B09001 in the variable column to see what the labels correspond to  
    # So the variables that we need are:
      # B9001_003: number_under_three
      # B9001_004: number_three_four
      # B9001_005: number_five
      # B9001_006: number_six_to_eight
    
  # These numbers are probably going to make the gap look worse than it actually is on the ground but this is the best estimate available based on differing     age ranges for different data sets 
    
   co_kids_count_eight_under <- get_acs(
        geography = "county",
        state = "CO",
        variables = c(number_under_three = "B09001_003", 
                      number_three_four = "B09001_004",
                      number_five = "B09001_005",
                      number_six_to_eight = "B09001_006"), 
        year = 2024
      )   
      
   
   # Export
      
     # co_kids_count_eight_under %>% write_csv("co_kids_count_eight_under.csv", na = "") 
   
   
# Add together 
   
   sum_co_kids_count_eight_under <- co_kids_count_eight_under %>% 
      group_by(NAME) %>% 
      summarise(sum_kids_eight_under = sum(estimate))
   
        # Check in SQL
   
          sqldf("SELECT name, sum(estimate)
            FROM co_kids_count_eight_under
            GROUP BY name
            ")
    
        # Matches so that's good          
          
    # Now I need the column names to match before subtracting but they have slightly different names. 
   
   renamed_sum_co_kids_count_eight_under <- sum_co_kids_count_eight_under %>%
      mutate(county = str_extract(NAME, ".*(?=\\s+[Cc]ounty)"))
   
# Now to join and calculate the number of gaps per county 
   
  spot_kids_gap <- renamed_sum_co_kids_count_eight_under %>% 
      full_join(pre_k_county_child_care_capacity, by='county') %>% 
      mutate(spot_kids_gap = sum_kids_eight_under - sum_pre_k_total_spots) 
   
  # Export
      
    spot_kids_gap %>% write_csv("spot_kids_gap.csv", na = "") 
     
      
```


```{r}

# Analyze more from the gaps 

  spot_kids_gap %>% 
  summarise(sum = sum(spot_kids_gap))

  # There's a gap of 498,816 kids per licensed spots, although a caveat is that the state has the number of licensed places in a facility in any given time. In theory kids could go to classes in the morning and afternoon if there were multiple offerings. 


```

```{r}

# Calculate rate of gap divided by total number of kids per county

spot_kids_gap %>% 
  mutate(gap_ratio = (spot_kids_gap/sum_kids_eight_under)*100)

# An additional story could be on why some of these counties have a better gap ratio than others. For ex, it looks like San Juan and San Miguel counties have most of their kids taken care of with the lowest gap ratio


```

```{r}

# Now doing similar calculations, but just for establishing a gap between kids 3 and under alongside the number of licensed spots JUST for them

co_kids_count_four_under <- get_acs(
        geography = "county",
        state = "CO",
        variables = c(number_under_three = "B09001_003", 
                      number_three_four = "B09001_004"), 
        year = 2024
      )   
 
    sum_co_kids_count_four_under <- co_kids_count_four_under %>% 
      group_by(NAME) %>% 
      summarise(sum_kids_four_under = sum(estimate))
   
        # Check in SQL
   
          sqldf("SELECT name, sum(estimate)
            FROM co_kids_count_four_under
            GROUP BY name
            ")
    
        # Matches so that's good          
          
    # Now I need the column names to match before subtracting but they have slightly different names. 
   
   renamed_sum_co_kids_count_four_under <- sum_co_kids_count_four_under %>%
      mutate(county = str_extract(NAME, ".*(?=\\s+[Cc]ounty)"))
  

```


```{r}
  # Need to recalculate just infant and toddler total spots to do new assessment

infant_toddler_total <-  licensed_child_care %>% 
      rowwise() %>% 
      mutate(infant_toddler_total = sum(c_across(licensed_center_infant_capacity:licensed_center_toddler_capacity), na.rm = TRUE))


infant_toddler_county_child_care_capacity <- infant_toddler_total %>% 
        group_by(county) %>% 
        summarise(sum_infant_toddler_total_spots = sum(infant_toddler_total, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
        arrange(sum_infant_toddler_total_spots)

# There are 5 counties without any licensed capacity for infants or toddlers. 

# Export
      
 infant_toddler_county_child_care_capacity %>% write_csv("infant_toddler_county_child_care_capacity.csv", na = "") 


```


```{r}


# Now to join and calculate the number of gaps per county 
   
  toddler_spot_kids_gap <- renamed_sum_co_kids_count_four_under %>% 
      full_join(infant_toddler_county_child_care_capacity, by='county') %>% 
      mutate(spot_kids_gap = sum_kids_four_under - sum_infant_toddler_total_spots) %>%
      mutate(gap_ratio = (spot_kids_gap/sum_kids_four_under)*100)
   
  # Export
      
    toddler_spot_kids_gap %>% write_csv("toddler_spot_kids_gap.csv", na = "") 


```


```{r}

# Just infant spots per county 
  # Because already a column with infant spots, don't need to make a new one with mutate

infant_county_child_care_capacity <- infant_toddler_total %>% 
        group_by(county) %>% 
        summarise(sum_infant_total_spots = sum(licensed_center_infant_capacity, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
        arrange(sum_infant_total_spots)

# There are 10 counties without any licensed capacity for infants. 

# Export
      
   infant_county_child_care_capacity %>% write_csv("infant_county_child_care_capacity.csv", na = "") 



```

```{r}

# Pull out just kids under three

co_kids_count_under_three <- get_acs(
        geography = "county",
        state = "CO",
        variables = c(number_under_three = "B09001_003"),
        year = 2024
      )   
 
    sum_co_kids_count_under_three <- co_kids_count_under_three %>% 
      group_by(NAME) %>% 
      summarise(sum_kids_under_3 = sum(estimate))
   
        # Check in SQL
   
          sqldf("SELECT name, sum(estimate)
            FROM co_kids_count_under_three
            GROUP BY name
            ")
    
        # Matches so that's good          
          
    # Now I need the column names to match before subtracting but they have slightly different names. 
  
   renamed_sum_co_kids_count_under_three <- sum_co_kids_count_under_three %>%
      mutate(county = str_extract(NAME, ".*(?=\\s+[Cc]ounty)"))



``` 


```{r}

# Join and check gap for infants

infant_spot_kids_gap <- renamed_sum_co_kids_count_under_three %>% 
      full_join(infant_county_child_care_capacity, by='county') %>% 
      mutate(spot_infant_gap = sum_kids_under_3 - sum_infant_total_spots) %>%
      mutate(gap_ratio = (spot_infant_gap/sum_kids_under_3)*100)

 # Export
      
    infant_spot_kids_gap %>% write_csv("infant_spot_kids_gap.csv", na = "") 



```




```{r}

# Number of licensed infant spots

infant_spot_kids_gap %>% 
  summarise(sum = sum(sum_infant_total_spots))

  # There are licensed spots for 9475 infants in Colorado.

    # Same as number in state data that I did in a pivot table to check this

# Sum total number of gaps

  infant_spot_kids_gap %>% 
  summarise(sum = sum(spot_infant_gap))

  # There's a gap of 173,544 infants per licensed spots, although a caveat is that the state has the number of licensed places in a facility in any given time. In theory kids could go to classes in the morning and afternoon if there were multiple offerings. And it is unclear how many infants need child care.

# Way to see all of these factors at once:
  
  infant_spot_kids_gap %>% 
  summarise(infants_total_statewide = sum(sum_kids_under_3),
             infant_spots_total_statewide = sum(sum_infant_total_spots),
             gap_total_statewide = sum(spot_infant_gap))

  # So there are 183,019 kids under three in CO. 
    # 9,475 licensed spots for kids 6 weeks to 18 months in CO. 
    # With a gap of 173,544 licensed spots for every infant under 3-years-old in the Centennial State. 

```
